title: Stopping junk mail in the UK
subtitle: Using the Mailing Preference Service, Royal Mail opt-out, and electoral register settings to reduce unsolicited post
excerpt: An average UK household receives 70-100 items of addressed direct mail and 50-80 unaddressed leaflets per year. Opting out of the main channels eliminates the majority within 3-4 months. This covers the four routes: Royal Mail door-to-door opt-out, Mailing Preference Service registration, the open electoral register, and unsubscribing from companies you have bought from.
sourceNotes: Direct Marketing Association (DMA) UK Mailing Preference Service guidance. Royal Mail door-to-door opt-out scheme documentation. Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) guidance on the electoral register and the open register. Citizens Advice guidance on stopping junk mail.

content.text: Junk mail in the UK arrives through two channels. First, unaddressed leaflets and catalogues delivered to every household on a round by Royal Mail or third-party distributors. Second, addressed direct mail sent to named individuals from purchased or compiled mailing lists. Each channel needs a different opt-out.

Royal Mail delivers unaddressed items (leaflets, menus, catalogues) to every house on a round as a commercial service. Registering an opt-out removes your address from those rounds. It does not cover leaflets delivered by private companies or volunteers such as estate agents, takeaways, or charity collectors.
Register via the Royal Mail website (search 'Royal Mail door-to-door opt-out'). Takes effect within 6 weeks of registration.
Re-registration is required if you move house; the opt-out applies to the address, not the individual.

The Mailing Preference Service is the UK's opt-out register for addressed direct mail. DMA member companies, which include the majority of large UK direct mailers, are required to screen their mailing lists against the MPS register before each send.
Register at mpsonline.org.uk. The process takes 5 minutes; registration is free. Takes full effect within 4 months as mailers update their list suppressions.
The MPS does not stop mail from companies you have an existing relationship with (companies you have bought from, charities you have donated to). These must be unsubscribed individually.
Non-DMA mailers and small local businesses may not check the MPS register. Mail from these sources continues regardless.
Route 3: electoral register open register opt-out

The electoral register has two versions. The full register is used only for elections and legal purposes. The open register is sold to businesses for direct mail. Your details go on the open register by default unless you opt out.
To opt out of the open register, contact your local Electoral Registration Office and request removal. This does not affect your right to vote or your presence on the full register.
The opt-out must be requested each year when the annual canvass form is returned, or by contacting the office directly after the canvass period.

Under UK GDPR, any company sending you direct mail must honour an unsubscribe request within 30 days. Write to the data controller (the address is usually on their privacy notice) stating you withdraw consent for direct marketing by post. Keep a copy of the letter. For catalogues, call the customer service line and request removal from the catalogue mailing list specifically.

attrs.symptom: Mail continues from a specific company 6 weeks after unsubscribing
attrs.cause: The company has not actioned the request within the 30-day statutory window, or the unsubscribe request did not reach the correct department
attrs.fix: Send a written unsubscribe request to the data controller by recorded post, referencing UK GDPR Article 21. If the mail continues after a further 30 days, file a complaint with the ICO (ico.org.uk).

attrs.symptom: Unaddressed leaflets continue despite Royal Mail opt-out
attrs.cause: The leaflets are distributed by a private company or individual, not via the Royal Mail door-to-door service
attrs.fix: Place a polite notice on the letterbox stating 'No unaddressed leaflets'. This has no legal force but is effective for most volunteer distributors. For persistent commercial distributors, contact the company directly.
